California
Fair P01itical

Practices Comrnission
IN RART
`聾繁亀馨嵩
」uly 8, 
■99■
Paul N. r'Pete" !,lccloskey, Jr.
california Foundation on the Environment
and the Economy
909 Montgomery Street, Third FIoor
San Francisco, CA 94133
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our Fi Ie No. I-91-263
Dear Mr. Mccloskey:
You have requested advice on behalf of the california
Foundation on the Environment and the Economy concerning the new
gift and honoraria Iimits of the Political Reforn Act (the
rrtr.gtr;.1 Since you have not narned a specific person with
responsibilities under the Act on whose behalf you have requested
this advice, we can only provide the followi.ng informal resPonse
to your question.2
Please be aware that the issues raised in this letter Present
significant policy questions which will be presented to the
Commission for their consideration in the future as a Proposed
regulation. 
In the meantime, ue have provided a conservative and
cautious interpretation of the Act.
OUEST IONS
1. Are payments for travel and accommodations provided to
elected officiaLs and designated employees by the' cal'ifornia
Foundation on the Environment and the Economy as part of the
foundation's study-travel prograrn gifts under the Act?
1 corr...r..nt Code Sections SIOOO-910f5. A1l statutory references
are to the Government code unless otherwise indicated' 
Commission
regulations appear at 2 California code of Regulations. Section
18500, et seo.- AII references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the california Code of Regulations'
provide the requestor with the
'or fornal srit€en advice. (section
2  .nfOrmal asS■Stance does not
immunity proVided by an opinion
83114F Regulation 
■8329(c)(3).)
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 95804Ю 807 0 (916)322″ 5660


our File No. A-9■
-263
Page 2
2. Under rrhat circumstances would the gift of travel and
accommodations be attributed to sponsors of the event, rather than
the caLifornia Foundation on the Environment and the Econotny, and
how would such an attribution affect the application of Section
89506?
CONCLUS I ONS
1. 
Pa)rments for travel and accommodations Provided to
elected officials 
and designated enployees by the california
Foundation on the Environment and the Economy as Part of the
foundation's study-travel progran would be exempted fron the gift
limits of the Act by section 89506. The payments would, however,
continue to be disclosable gifts and could result in potential
conflicts of interest.
2. The palrments for the travel and accommodations would be a
gift from the various sponsors if the sponsors of the conference
inew or had reason to know at the time their donations are made
which public officials, 
or which identifiabte group of public
officiils. 
wiIl receive the gifts of travel that the california
Foundation on the Environment and the Economy uttimately provides'
Since the sponsors are not charitable or educational nonprofit
organizations. Section 89506 would not appIy.
FACTS
The California Foundation on the Environment and the EconoDy
("CFEE") is a nonprofit charitable corporation exeBpt frolu
taxation under Internal Revenue code Section 501(c) (l). 
CFEE l{as
created in 1979 to bring together business, 1abor, cornmunity, and
environmental leaders with public officials, 
policy nakers and
expert academicians. in forums designed to address complex
economic and social issues.
CFEE generalJ.y holds two to three california conferences and
one foreign study-Lravel proiect each year. various public
officials 
are invited to each event by CFEE to participate.
CFEE's president, subject to ratification 
by the Board of
Directois, has sole discretion over the subject Batter of its
conferences and study-travel progran, where they wiII take pIace,
and who wiLl be invited to parti-ipate. 
CFEE's governj.ng board
consists of 30 members who serve on a voluntary basis. only one
member of the board of directors is a public official-
CFEE finances the various events rrith an endownent froD a
private foundation, and contributions solicited from businesses
lprincipally 
corporations), associations, and public.agencies
hiving in intereit in the subject Eatter of the particular seuinar
or study-traveJ. project CFEE is holding. Donors :uay participate
in the events, at their oun expense.


Our File No. A-9■
-263
Page 3
CFEE has planned a study― travel project to Europe COncerning
env■ronmental control technology and alternatiVe energy for
september′  ■99■.  Participants 
■nclude members of the European
commun■ty′  executives and staff of the European Co■
lmun■ty

i[:nT「
3iteE:ettglttg:[1。「X::r8:li::lRIl° nS・  In
legislators and legislative staff to participate in the European
study― travel program.  CFEE plans tO pay for traVel and
acco■lmodatiOns of the legislators.
ANALYSIS
section 89504(a)proVides′
 in pertinent part:
(a)No elected state officer shall accept
gifts from any single source in any calendar year
with a total value of more than tWo hundred fifty
dollars (S250).
(b) ThiS Section shall not prohibit or limit
the fol10Wing:
(1)  Payments, advances′
 Or
reimbursements for travel and related lodging
and subS■Stence permitted by Section 89506.
recei3::tよ°
:i:こ
5::(:[1↓と
lit:[:こi:la:95::r:。it::呈き
i[h:[[:[ittle 9.ft
is subject to the new 9■ft limit.  SectiOn 39506 provides:
(a) Payments, advances′  or reimbursementS,
for travel′  including actual transpOrtation and
related lodging and subs■stence whiCh iS reasonablv
chapter if either of the folloWing apply:
(1) The travel j,s in connection with a
speech g j,ven by the elected state officer,
mernber of a state board or comrnission, or
designated e]nployee of a state agency, the
).odging and subsistence expenses are limited
to the day imrnediately preceding, the day of,
and the day immediately folloering the speech,
and the travel is within the United States.
(2) The travel is provided by a
governtrent, a governDental agency, a foreign
government, a governmental authorj.ty, a bona
fide public or private educational
j.nstitution, as defined in Section 203 of the
Revenue and Taxation Code, or a nonprofit
charitable or reliqious orqanization which is


our File No. A-91-263
Page 4
exempt from taxation under Section 501(c) (3)
of the Internal Revenue Code, or by a Person
domiciled outside the United states which
substantial)-y satisfies the requirements for
tax exempt status under section 50L(c) (3) of
the Internal Revenue code.
section 89506 (a) , ernPhasis
added .
It appears that the activity described in your letter is
reasonably related to an issue of state, national, or
internati6nal public policy pursuant to the exception in Section
89506. You stated that the purpose of the EuroPean conference was
to discuss environnental control technology and alternative
energy. Thus while the exception in section 89506(a) (I-) night
appli to payments for travel made by CFEE if the conference was to
b;- hefd in Lfre continental United States and the officials 
were to
make speeches, it wilL not apply to a conference in Europe where
the officials wil,l not be naking speeches'
Section 89506(a) (2), however, provides an additional
exception to the new gif! and honoraria restrictions of the Act'
ror ihe exception in subdivision (a)(2) to apply, the travel must
be reasonably related to a Iegislative or governmental purpose, or
to an issue of state, nationai, or international public policy and
be paid for by a nonProfit charitable or religious organization
whiah is exenpt from taxation under Section 501(c) (l).
You stated that CFEE is a tax exempt nonprofit charitabLe
organization under section 5o1(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code.
Thrls, where cFEE is the source oi tire qift, the gifts of.travel
ttrat' y 
l r.rithin the excePtion in
secti6n 89506(a) (2) and bL- exempted from the gift and honoraria
Linits of the Act. Please note, the payments sould stil'l 
need to
be disclosed and may result in a conflict of interest at a future
tine.
However, under some circumstances it is appropriate to pierce
through one organization wh j.ch aPPears to be the source of a gift,
to the actual donors of funds used to Provide the gift. 
For
exanple, in the BeIl Advice Letter (Advice Letter No. A-91-077,
copy'enclosedl , we advised that where an orqanizatsion was actj'ng
as-l u,"re i.ntermediary for gifts, piercing was appropriate.
According to your facts, CFEE's president, subject to
ratification 
by th- board of directors, has sole discretion over
the subject Datter of its conferences and study-travel progra[,
where they trill. take place, and nho wiII be invited to
participale. 
However, CFEE finances the various events with an
endowment from a private foundation, and donations solicited fron
businesses, associations, and public agencies having an interest
in ttre =uu:ect Eatter of the plrticular 
seminar or study-travel


Our File NO. A-91-263
Page 5
project CFEE is holding. Donors tnay also participate in the
events at their own expense.
In the context of contribution Iinits and disclosure,
piercing through an intermediary was appropriate where the true
source of the funds had provided a contribution to another entity
and designated the candidate \rho lras to receive the contribution.
(wilson Advice Letter, No. A-79-077, copy attached. ) Under those
circumstances the person acting as the interrnediary was not
treated as the source of the contribution.3
consistent with this reasoning. we conclude that vhere the
sponsors of the conference knou or have reason to know at the time
they nake their donations vhich public officials, 
or vhich
identifiable qroup of public officials will receive the gifts of
travel that cFEE ultimately provides, the sponsors are the true
source of the gift. 
For example, j.f the conference will be on a
topic of which only a few Iegislators tould appropriately attend,
the sponsors will have reason to know who will be invited.
conversely, uhere the topic is one that any legislator may be
invited to attend, and there is no past pattern to indicate that a
certain IegisJ.ator or selective group will be invited, piercing
may not be appropr iate .
we do not have sufficient facts to determine whether the
sponsors have reason to know r.rho wil.l be invited. 
However. if
they do, the sponsors will be the true source of the gifts to the
public officials, 
and section 89506(a) (2) uould not exemDt the
qifts from the limits of the Act.
I trust thj.s letter has addressed your concerns. should you
have any further questions regardi.ng this matter please feel free
to contact ne at (916) 322-5901 .
S incere Iy ,
Scott Ha l Iabr in
Acting ceneral counse I
SH:―
:aa
Enclosures
3 rhi. contrasts with the situation where a person provides a
contribution to a potitical 
committee which in turn uses those
funds to rnake a contribution to a candidate selected so1ely by the
comrnittee. Under those circunstances rre have said that the
cornmittee has received a contribution fron the donor and that the
candi.date had received a contribution frorn the conmittee.
(Section 85302. )
John w. WaI Iace
Counsel, Legal Divis i
